tweets.text:
The reason we see so many people with repeat payouts is because our rules are more trader friendly than other firms. 

We do not use trailing max drawdown. 

At most firms your safety net trails your balance up. Take a payout, your balance drops, and that floor is sitting right below you. 

One bad day, you're out. 

So everyone's scared to withdraw. 

Ours is set once and never moves. 

Profit, withdraw, repeat, the floor doesn't move. 

There’s never been a better time to get started with Breakout. 

Use code MAYNE at checkout for a discount.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Anyone can yolo a funded account into one payout.

But how many of you are hitting the wire on repeat?

More than a quarter of the traders we paid this year got paid 5+ times.

Fair rules, repeat winners. Funny how that works.
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
If you're curious, $MSTR becomes effectively worthless at $25K Bitcoin.

Strategy has $22.2B of debt and preferred shares senior to the commons (not including the dividend obligations).

846,842*$25K = $21.2B + $1.1B (cash reserve) = $22.3B.

That's the power of leverage!
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Even if Bitcoin fell to $25,000 tomorrow, stayed there until 2036, and somehow Strategy never raised another dollar, it would still have enough capital to make all dividend payments.

If Bitcoin is $25,000 in 2036, I’d admit my thesis was wrong.

Of course I don’t expect this.

thread_tweets.full_text:
If you're curious, $MSTR becomes effectively worthless at $25K Bitcoin.

Strategy has $22.2B of debt and preferred shares senior to the commons (not including the dividend obligations).

846,842*$25K = $21.2B + $1.1B (cash reserve) = $22.3B.

thread_tweets.text:
It gets worse when you consider Strategy has to come up with $10B+ cash sometime in the next 12-24 months to pay their convertible debt + dividend obligations.

They'd either have to sell 400K - 500K BTC at those price levels, or sell a TON of MSTR shares ATM a price that would already be in the gutter... it'll probably be some combination of both - and either one would have an incredibly dilutive effect on MSTR share holders.

thread_tweets.text:
Fair -

but 2022 was also completely different structurally

The debt was ~10% the size it is today, converts years further out, and it was basically free to carry (~$40M/yr).

The software cash flow could easily cover the payments and represented a much larger % of the actual debt - so the Bitcoin didn't have to be touched to service it.

In 2022 the option was practically free to hold.

In 2026 holding it costs billions of dollars per year plus a near term debt wall.

Also, in 2022 they only held ~0.7% of the total BTC circulating supply vs. ~4.2% today.

They're a much larger structural risk to BTC itself if they actually need to sell a decent portion for cash.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Fair -

but 2022 was also completely different structurally

The debt was ~10% the size it is today, converts years further out, and it was basically free to carry (~$40M/yr).

The software cash flow could easily cover the payments and represented a much larger % of the actual debt - so the Bitcoin didn't have to be touched to service it.

In 2022 the option was practically free to hold.

In 2026 holding it costs billions of dollars per year plus a near term debt wall.

Also, in 2022 they only held ~0.7% of the total BTC circulating supply vs. ~4.2% today.

They're a much larger structural risk to BTC itself if they actually need to sell a decent portion for cash.

tweets.full_text:
If you're curious, $MSTR becomes effectively worthless at $25K Bitcoin.

Strategy has $22.2B of debt and preferred shares senior to the commons (not including the dividend obligations).

846,842*$25K = $21.2B + $1.1B (cash reserve) = $22.3B.

That's the power of leverage!

It gets worse when you consider Strategy has to come up with $10B+ cash sometime in the next 12-24 months to pay their convertible debt + dividend obligations.

They'd either have to sell 400K - 500K BTC at those price levels, or sell a TON of MSTR shares ATM a price that would already be in the gutter... it'll probably be some combination of both - and either one would have an incredibly dilutive effect on MSTR share holders.

@proofofworkmaxi brother - if the government "bails them out" and is the counterparty to that transaction, *maybe* they'll have a chance at saving the dividend but you better believe they don't care about saving MSTR holders in the process lol

honestly just want to save as many people as I can from this ponzi before it fully implodes so that they can have capital for the run back up

I think STRC holders will continue to get paid out, but here's the key:

I think it's very unlikely that $100 "peg" is ever restored without them doing something drastic (and sacrificing MSTR / BTC in the process) which I still think would have a hard time getting it back close to par

@BigSlick780 I believe in Bitcoin and believe in this industry

but MSTR / STRC is a ponzi that I believe is about to hurt a lot of innocent people, so I'm trying to help as many people as I can wake up to their bs before it happens

Fair -

but 2022 was also completely different structurally

The debt was ~10% the size it is today, converts years further out, and it was basically free to carry (~$40M/yr).

The software cash flow could easily cover the payments and represented a much larger % of the actual debt - so the Bitcoin didn't have to be touched to service it.

In 2022 the option was practically free to hold.

In 2026 holding it costs billions of dollars per year plus a near term debt wall.

Also, in 2022 they only held ~0.7% of the total BTC circulating supply vs. ~4.2% today.

They're a much larger structural risk to BTC itself if they actually need to sell a decent portion for cash.
